source.title: A high-throughput multiparameter screen for accelerated development and optimization of soluble genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors

initial_request.research_problem: Developing and optimizing soluble genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors is challenging due to low-throughput, single-parameter screening methods.
initial_request.known_context: Existing biosensor screens are limited in the number of conditions that can be tested, hindering multiparameter optimization.
quantifiable_goals: Increase screening throughput and content by orders of magnitude
Develop a lactate biosensor with large response and low pH sensitivity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing biosensor screens are limited in the number of conditions that can be tested, hindering multiparameter optimization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to increase screening throughput and enable multiparameter optimization for biosensor engineering.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining droplet microfluidics and automated two-photon fluorescence lifetime imaging (2p-FLIM) will increase screening content and throughput by orders of magnitude.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The BeadScan platform was designed to combine droplet microfluidics and automated 2p-FLIM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address low protein expression in initial GSB method.

evidence.quote_or_span: existing screens are still limited in the number of conditions that can be tested against the biosensor

we have surmounted these limitations, increasing screening content and throughput by orders of magnitude, by combining droplet microfluidics and automated two-photon fluorescence lifetime imaging (2p-FLIM)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial GSB method yields nanomolar protein expression, insufficient for direct fluorescence detection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Protein expression levels are too low for biosensor detection without enzymatic amplification.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Increasing DNA copy number per bead will boost protein expression to detectable levels.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attempt existing bead PCR methods to achieve high copy numbers of full-length amplicons.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Existing bead PCR methods failed to reliably produce beads loaded with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons; only 0.1–1% of the resulting DNA beads were capable of driving strong expression of target sensors in IVTT droplets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a new bead PCR method to overcome these limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The enzymes are at nanomolar levels in the GSBs and their activity is visualized with fluorogenic substrates; but without the advantage of enzymatic amplification in the assay, fluorescent biosensors are difficult to detect at these low concentrations.

In our hands, these methods failed to reliably produce beads loaded with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons; only 0.1–1% of the resulting DNA beads were capable of driving strong expression of our target sensors in IVTT droplets.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing bead PCR methods failed to produce high-copy DNA beads.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a reliable method to generate beads with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Amplifying DNA free in solution in droplets and then immobilizing on streptavidin beads will yield high copy numbers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a new bead PCR method: amplify DNA free in solution in droplets with biotinylated 3' primer, then immobilize on streptavidin beads via microfluidic merging.
evolution_trajectory.observation: New method achieved >200,000 copies per bead.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize DNA loading to prevent protein aggregates.

evidence.quote_or_span: To overcome these limitations, we instead allowed template DNA to be amplified free in solution in droplets containing a biotinylated 3' primer, and then subsequently immobilized the PCR products on streptavidin beads.
we chose to immobilize the amplified DNA on beads, purify the DNA beads and then use a minimal volume to deliver them into IVTT droplets

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High-copy DNA beads achieved, but risk of protein aggregates.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Uncontrolled DNA loading may cause protein aggregation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Limiting DNA loading to ~100,000 copies by pre-blocking streptavidin sites and using limiting biotinylated 3' primer will prevent aggregates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DNA loading limited to ~100,000 copies by pre-blocking streptavidin sites; limiting biotinylated 3' primer ensures only fully extended amplicons are captured.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select a scaffold for lactate biosensor.

evidence.quote_or_span: we used a limiting concentration of the 3' primer to ensure the complete extension of all biotinylated primer molecules, so that only fully extended amplicons are captured on the streptavidin beads.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High-copy DNA beads ready for library screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a suitable scaffold for a lactate biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TlpC dCACHE domain may undergo large conformational change upon lactate binding, making it a good scaffold.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select TlpC as scaffold and fuse with T-Sapphire (TS) to create TlpC-TS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TlpC selected as an untested scaffold; reasoning that lactate binding may generate a large conformational change between N- and C-termini.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected an untested scaffold for our biosensor: the extracellular dCACHE domain of the bacterial chemotaxis protein, TlpC from H. pylori.
we reasoned that lactate binding may generate a large conformational change between the N- and C- termini, which are in close proximity

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal linker composition and length unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening a large linker library will identify variants with large lactate-induced lifetime changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate a library of 49,152 TlpC-TS linker variants and screen using BeadScan.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two high-contrast biosensors isolated (TlpC-TS #1537 and #1059) with inverted lifetime changes (ΔLT = 0.2 ns and 0.3 ns) and physiological affinities (Kapp = 0.19 ± 0.13 mM and 0.66 ± 0.11 mM).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize pH sensitivity of TlpC-TS variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated a library of TlpC-TS biosensor variants that sampled 49,152 different linkers, varying both length and composition
Initial screen of the TlpC-TS lactate biosensor library, showing data from individual biosensor variants captured in GSBs.
Average photon counts within each ROI are plotted against the maximum change in lifetime between 0 and 10 mM lactate; each data point represents a single GSB.
Two GSBs with large lifetime changes were selected (dotted boxes).
5, 90 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl and combined with Ni-NTA nanospheres (Kisker-biotech, PPS-0.2NI-NTA) and sterile 50% glycerol to final concentrations of 1.
Performance of TlpC-TS #1059 at four different pH values. Dose-response curves were collected on a uniform sample of GSBs expressing TlpC-TS #1059 (n = 96 GSBs, mean ± SD).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: TlpC-TS variants may be sensitive to pH, limiting quantitative measurements.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TlpC-TS is pH-sensitive within physiological ranges.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TlpC-TS #1059 showed sensitivity to pH changes within physiologically relevant ranges, a limitation for quantitative lactate measurements.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Switch fluorophore to reduce pH sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we found that TlpC-TS was highly specific for its cognate ligand over other chemically similar ligands, but was also sensitive to pH changes within physiologically relevant ranges
Performance of TlpC-TS #1059 at four different pH values. Dose-response curves were collected on a uniform sample of GSBs expressing TlpC-TS #1059 (n = 96 GSBs, mean ± SD).
We therefore set out to reduce the pH responses of our prototype lactate biosensor to enable more accurate quantitation of intracellular lactate concentrations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a pH-resistant fluorophore to reduce pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Replacing T-Sapphire with mTurquoise2, a pH-resistant cyan fluorescent protein, will reduce pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Switch fluorophore to mTurquoise2 and re-screen linker library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TlpC-TQ linker library generated with mTurquoise2; multiparameter screening comparing lactate and pH responses for 1411 variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Isolate variant with large lactate response and low pH sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Multiparameter screening of a linker library of TlpC-TQ, comparing lifetime changes in response to lactate versus pH for each of 1411 individual GSBs.

response may arise from the T-Sapphire fluorescent protein $^{46}$ , and we repeated our linker library using mTurquoise2, a highly pH-resistant fluorophore recently used to develop a fluorescent lifetime sensor for calcium levels $^{7}$ , which is also brighter and more photostable than T-Sapphire $^{7,14,46}$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify a variant with large lactate response and greatly diminished pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiparameter screening will isolate rare variants that resist the correlation between lactate and pH responses.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen TlpC-TQ library and select variant #1083.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variant TlpC-TQ #1083 (renamed LiLac) isolated with large lactate response and greatly diminished pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate LiLac in mammalian cells and brain slices.

evidence.quote_or_span: Multiparameter screening of a linker library of TlpC-TQ, comparing lifetime changes in response to lactate versus pH for each of 1411 individual GSBs.
Nevertheless, out of 1,411 variants we successfully isolated a rare variant, TlpC-TQ #1083, that displayed large lactate responses and greatly diminished pH sensitivity (Fig.
The pH-resistant variant (#1083, top) was renamed LiLac.
Despite a general correlation between lactate- and pH-induced responses, rare variants that resisted this trend could be isolated (#1083).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate LiLac performance in cellular environments.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: LiLac will show high uniformity, sensitivity, and outperform Laconic in mammalian cells and brain slices.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate LiLac in HEK293T cells and acute hippocampal brain slices.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LiLac showed high uniformity, sensitivity, and outperformed Laconic with 6-fold larger response and SNR ~25; also >40% intensity change.

evidence.quote_or_span: Lifetime values are highly uniform across a population of cells exposed to a single concentration of lactate and are responsive to changes in lactate concentration in the external bath solution
LiLac yields highly reproducible measurements of intracellular lactate concentrations across a population of cells and is sensitive to even modest manipulations of extracellular lactate (1 to 2 mM)
the lifetime of the Laconic donor species varies widely across cells bathed in lactate
the high precision of the LiLac readout suggests that the cell-to-cell variability observed with Laconic may be an artifact of the biosensor

In mammalian cells, the lifetime response from LiLac outperforms that of Laconic, the first and most widely used genetically encoded lactate biosensor, exhibiting a 6-fold larger response size and high sensitivity (SNR \~25).

In cells, LiLac displays a \~6-fold larger response size, >5-fold reduced variability, and substantially higher signal to noise ratio (LiLac $_{SNR}$ \~ 25, Laconic $_{donor\ lifetime,SNR}$ < 1; Suppl.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Confirm that LiLac's in vitro performance matches cellular performance, indicating high specificity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: LiLac's in vitro and cellular performance similarity suggests high specificity for lactate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare calibration in permeabilized cells with IVTT-produced GSBs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Calibration of LiLac in permeabilized HEK293T cells was remarkably similar to that in IVTT-produced GSBs, suggesting high specificity for lactate.

evidence.quote_or_span: calibration of the LiLac response in permeabilized HEK293T cells was remarkably similar to that in IVTT-produced GSBs
the similarity of LiLac's performance in vitro and in cells suggests that the LiLac response is indeed highly specific for lactate in the context of the cellular environment

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: BeadScan has limitations: cannot evaluate kinetics within GSBs, and is most appropriate for soluble biosensors.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: These limitations do not detract from the demonstrated utility of BeadScan for soluble biosensor development.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BeadScan cannot evaluate biosensor kinetics within GSBs due to unknown ligand diffusion rates; most appropriate for soluble biosensors, not membrane-associated or GPCR-based biosensors.

evidence.quote_or_span: We are currently unable to evaluate biosensor kinetics within the GSB environment, which would require a precise understanding of ligand diffusion rates across the polyelectrolyte shell.
BeadScan is also most appropriate for screening soluble biosensors, not membrane-associated or GPCR-based biosensors

success_verification.final_conclusion: LiLac is a high-performance lactate biosensor with large response, low pH sensitivity, and high uniformity in cells, outperforming Laconic. BeadScan enables rapid multiparameter optimization of soluble biosensors.
limitations: Cannot evaluate biosensor kinetics within GSBs
Most appropriate for soluble biosensors, not membrane-associated or GPCR-based
